essays:
Essay A — Psychology & Education UCAS PS

Knowing how education inequality can be compounded with other difficulties and form a vicious cycle has concerned me about the wellbeing and development of disadvantaged children, whether economically deprived or with special educational needs. My aspiration to improve their education outcomes compelled me to study Psychology and Education to acquire a scientific understanding of the mechanisms behind learning and research the cognitive development of this particular population.

To comprehensively understand the education environments in disadvantaged communities, I went to Ya'an as a volunteer teacher this summer. Most children here are left behind when their parents migrate to work, so I taught them emotion regulation and physical protection strategies to mitigate the pain of growing up alone. Throughout my teaching, a pair of 11-year-old twins caught my attention by their poor academic performance and extreme silence. Their distress prompted me to spend more time with them, and I gained their trust after teaching them how to read characters of a song lyric and singing with them. It was satisfying to witness their growth, and I hope to apply developmental psychology theories that I will learn to create classroom interventions and a teacher training program for future volunteer teachers in Ya'an.

Not only children from low-income families experience education inequities, but also children with disabilities. My cousin is autistic and has not gone to school since third grade. While he fits into no usual school practices, my aunt is too conservative to admit his symptoms and seek special education for him. Her opinions provoked me to explore academic articles on how to interact with autistic children as a caregiver and advised her to use positive reinforcement since autistic children respond best to compliments, as well as to engage in physical activities with him in addition to conversations.

In my Global Perspectives class, I wrote a paper on the relationship between prescription drug use and high school dropout rates. In addition to data presented by WHO and CDC, I interviewed eight teenagers addicted to antidepressants to collect qualitative data. It was challenging to establish a rapport with the participants, but I strengthened our relationships by hours of conversations. I realized that many of them were led to drug use by academic and social pressures and became addicted as it fulfills their desires to escape from reality, and they dropped out eventually.

---

Essay B — Short paragraphs #1 #2 #3

My earliest interest in psychology stemmed from observing my 3-year-old brother. He often procrastinated before meals, but after I praised him for sitting at the table on time, he gradually developed the habit and even began helping to prepare. This vivid example of positive reinforcement showed me how psychological principles can shape behavior in meaningful ways.

Later, in high school, I became increasingly aware of how psychology could also support education and well-being. I noticed that many classmates struggled with academic pressure and peer conflicts. I tried to apply the psychology theories I learned from book to help. For instance, I used cognitive reappraisal strategies to guide friends through interpersonal difficulties.

These small efforts showed me how psychology can connect theory with practical support for learning and growth. They inspired me to study psychology and psychology with education, so that I can contribute to young people's development both academically and emotionally.

My AP Psychology studies gave me my first experience of linking theory with research and educational practice. I investigated how parenting styles influence children's fairness awareness. Initially, I used PSDQ questionnaires for parents of 60 fourth-graders, but a low response rate led me to adapt the method with a child-focused version — a practical revision that taught me the value of flexibility in research design. In the experiment, children evaluated scenarios like candy distribution games on a 5-point fairness scale. ANOVA results showed that those with authoritative parents best recognised fair allocation and least accepted selfish outcomes.

My EPQ explored how parenting styles influence children's empathy through reviewing over twenty studies and examining factors like emotion socialisation. By assessing tools such as the IRI and BES, I concluded that authoritative parenting best supports empathy development due to its emotional responsiveness, though biological, cultural, and contextual factors also play key roles. Meanwhile, this academic research experience not only enhance my research proficiency, but also reinforced my determination to pursue further studies in psychology.

I co-founded the MHC Psychology Club with three classmates and served as vice president. The club has 30-40 members and meets weekly. I am responsible for incorporating psychology knowledge into real-life examples. In regular meetings, we shared psychology knowledge closely related to student life. For example, we taught students how developmental theories of emotion regulation suggest strategies such as distraction or talking to friends after conflicts.

I also organized experiential activities such as sand table exercises and lacquer fan making. The sand table allowed students to express and release suppressed emotions, while the lacquer fan activity cultivated concentration, reduced anxiety, and introduced traditional craftsmanship. Through this experience, my collaboration and communication skills were elevated.

---

Essay C — UCAS New Format Q1/Q2/Q3

My desire to study psychology stems from a fascination with how psychological principles explain human thought and emotion and can be applied to enhance wellbeing. In 2023, I founded the Human Observation Centre to provide a discussion platform for peers interested in human behaviour. Each week, we read academic papers and related them to everyday experiences. For instance, when introducing Hobson and McCarley's Activation-Synthesis Theory of dreaming, we analysed a member's dream of being trapped in a maze as the brain's attempt to process emotional stress rather than hidden symbolism. Helping members begin to use mindfulness to reflect on emotions and better understand themselves, I realised this fulfilment of helping others drove me to further explore psychology.

Motivated by the sense of purpose gained from supporting others, I became increasingly aware of how prevalent stress was among my peers. Some found comfort through exercise, which sparked my curiosity about why physical activity could improve one's mood. Reading about the endorphin hypothesis offered an initial explanation, yet I felt it overlooked the other psychological dimensions. To explore this further, I conducted a project on the relationship between exercise and general mood during the Oxford Summer School on Experimental Psychology. By refining on existing questionnaire and analysing participants' responses, I found evidence supporting the hypothesis while also revealing its limits. Drawing on self-efficacy or social interaction theories, I proposed a more comprehensive view. This experience improved my critical thinking skills and taught me to be Investigative rather than passive in learning.

It also made me realise that emotional well-being is influenced by intertwined factors. Eager to understand this complexity, I turned to investigate social media usage as a critical factor of improving general mood. For my EPQ, I explored whether online communication strengthens or weakens friendships, approaching the topic with critical awareness. Instead of seeking a simple conclusion, I evaluated contrasting evidence: while online platforms can foster self-disclosure and intimacy, they would also breed risks of misunderstanding due to lack of non-verbal cues.

In summer 2024, I joined a 10-day volunteer teaching programme in rural Yunnan, where I taught writing skills and concepts such as cognitive inertia. By listening carefully and valuing students' responses, I created a safe space where they could speak more freely and confidently. During home visits, I observed local girls often faced low social status due to traditions norms, which constrained their identity development and fostered feelings of inferiority.

---

Essay D — CTB / Menstruation / Iowa Gambling Task

When I was in grade 10, I participated in a project competition called China Thinks Big which encourages teenagers to find social issues and solutions. Our group chose the social attitude towards LGBTQ+ and will it influence the mental states of LGBTQ+ themselves. We made a questionnaire and a lot of random interviews to test the social attitudes and some specific interviews of the people belonging to the LGBTQ+ group about their opinions. The result turned out that social attitude still didn't come out as our most expected result. Therefore, we planned an offline activity that tried to reduce the demonization of the LGBTQ+ group. That was the first sparkle that fired my love for psychology.

I started to focus more on the gender topic because of this experience. I raised my interest in menstruation shame when I was in Grade 11. It all started once when my friend forgot to bring a sanitary pad and asked me to borrow some. I realized that there is still an unseen rule that requires menstruation not to be seen, talked or even acknowledged, although it is a natural biological process. After knowing all these adverse effects, I was determined to change this situation. I made some suggestions to the school to install a box to provide pads in the girls' bathroom. To break the rule that menstruation cannot be mentioned in public, I gave a speech to the whole school. After my speech, I received so many positive comments, especially from girls.

This year, I replicated a psychology study with two team members in our school as a project for my psychology lessons, and I chose the Iowa gambling task. However, instead of detecting problems in patients with damage to the ventromedial prefrontal cortex, I decided to focus on the gender differences on how to process the risk during the gambling task. I prepared both the online version of the task and the actual cards for participants to choose how they will do the gambling task. Although our study has not been done yet, my prediction is that the people with a younger age will tend to make a worse choice than the elder people in the task based on the relevant paper I read.
